text: Welcome to Distributed Systems! Lecture notes for Week 1 are posted in Materials. Please skim DDIA Chapter 1 before our first Zoom session on Wednesday. Auditors are welcome to join all live sessions.
Week 1 slides are up. Office hours this term are Tuesdays 5-6pm PT on Zoom with me and Thursdays 4-5pm PT with our TA Priya. Bring questions on the system model assumptions.
Lab 1 (Vector Clocks in Go) is released and due March 26. Starter repo and the test harness are linked in Materials. Push to your own private fork and submit the link.
Reminder: the Lamport timestamps quiz closes Friday night. It is short, just one conceptual question. Re-read the Time and Clocks lecture notes if you are unsure about total vs causal order.

This week we tackle consensus. I have posted both the Raft extended paper and Lamport's Paxos Made Simple in Materials. Read Raft first, it will make Paxos click. Lab 2 (Raft leader election) drops Monday.
No live lecture the week of October 4. Use the time to make progress on Lab 2. I have recorded a walkthrough of the election timeout logic and posted it in Materials.
Lab 2 deadline extended to October 17 by popular request. Common bug I am seeing: not resetting the election timer on a valid AppendEntries. Check the TA notes in Materials.

Final project proposals are due November 7. Keep scope realistic for the time we have left. A small replicated KV store or a CRDT-backed counter are both great choices. Email me if you want to talk through an idea.
Great term, everyone. Final project demos are the week of November 17. Grades and feedback for the MapReduce review are posted. Auditors, thanks for the thoughtful discussion this summer.
Welcome to Advanced Go! Make sure you are on Go 1.22 or later. First warmup assignment is posted. We meet live Mondays 6-7:30pm PT and code reviews happen async in the repo.
This module is all about goroutines and channels. Slides and a curated set of Go blog posts on concurrency patterns are in Materials. The worker pool assignment is your first real project.
Heads up: the memory model quiz is open this week. It is conceptual, no code. Re-read the happens-before section of the Go memory model spec linked in Materials.
Context-aware HTTP client assignment is live. The most common mistake is leaking goroutines when the context is cancelled. Use go test -race and watch for it. Office hours Wednesday 5pm PT.
Generics module is posted. Read the type parameters proposal sections I linked. For the assignment, prefer the simplest constraint that compiles, do not over-engineer the type set.
Last module: profiling and performance. The pprof assignment is posted. Capture a CPU profile and a heap profile. I want to see the flame graph in your writeup, not just the numbers.
Welcome to Cloud Architecture! Set up your AWS free-tier account this week and enable a billing alarm so you do not get surprised. Unit 1 slides and the EC2 lab are posted.
Networking week. The VPC design assignment is up. Diagrams can be hand-drawn or use the draw.io template in Materials. Live session Thursday covers NAT gateways and security groups.
Terraform module assignment released, due October 2. Pin your provider versions and commit your state to a remote backend, not locally. Example backend config is in Materials.
Final unit: Kubernetes on EKS. The autoscaling assignment is posted and due October 16. Tear down your cluster when you are done to avoid charges. Midterm feedback is also out.
Welcome to Database Systems Internals! We lean heavily on DDIA Chapters 3, 7, and 9. Install PostgreSQL 16 locally this week. First reading response is posted.
The B-tree index assignment is the biggest project of the term, due December 13. Start early. I posted a reference reading on page layout and a slide deck on LSM vs B-tree tradeoffs in Materials.
Query planning week. The EXPLAIN ANALYZE assignment is live. Bring a query you are stuck on to office hours Tuesday 5pm PT and we will read the plan together.
Transactions and isolation this week. The MVCC assignment is posted and due October 11. Read the snapshot isolation section carefully, write skew trips up almost everyone the first time.

Reminder: MVCC assignment is due this Saturday. Next week we move into replication and distributed data, where this course and the distributed systems material really converge. Reading is DDIA Chapter 9.
